methods.description: This method describes the process for selecting sampling stations, collecting sediment samples using a Van Veen Grab and core samplers, measuring environmental factors, processing samples (staining, washing, drying, separation using carbon tetrachloride), and counting/identifying organisms (Foraminifera and Ostracods).

steps.description: totally 20 stations were selected in 5 line transects (Table I).
steps.searchable_summary: Select sampling stations based on transects

steps.description: Environmental factors like the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO), water salinity (WS), pH, electric conductivity (EC), water temperature (WT) the organic content, sediment grain size, heavy metals content (copper, lead and cadmium) as well as species richness and abundance were measured in sediment samples
steps.searchable_summary: Measure environmental factors at sampling stations
outputs: Environmental data (DO, WS, pH, EC, WT, organic content, grain size, heavy metals)
Species richness and abundance data (measured in samples)

steps.description: sediment samples taken from four different vertical heights of 5, 10, 15, 20 m using Van Veen Grab 100 cm².
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment samples using a Van Veen Grab at specified depths

steps.description: Sampling events were repeated three times during the sampling season.
steps.searchable_summary: Repeat sampling events multiple times
outputs: Repeated sets of sediment samples and environmental data

steps.description: To make an understanding from microbenthic communities of the sampling stations, five samples were taken using core samplers of 3.5 cm diameter and 7 cm height.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment core samples for microbenthic communities
inputs: Core samplers (3.5 cm diameter, 7 cm height)

steps.description: Since sediments are frequently heterogeneous we tried to take several sub-samples and mixed them together in a composite sample (Darnley et al., 1995).
steps.searchable_summary: Create composite samples by mixing sub-samples

steps.description: Sampled sediments were stored in plastic jars and kept under the room temperature before taking to the lab.
steps.searchable_summary: Store sediment samples

steps.description: To study meiobenthic specimens, the sediment samples were exposed to the Rose Bengal stain for at least three weeks before lab analysis (Lutze and Altenbach, 1991).
steps.searchable_summary: Stain sediment samples with Rose Bengal

steps.description: In the lab sediment samples were washed using 63 µm sieve.
steps.searchable_summary: Wash sediment samples using a sieve

steps.description: Remaining material was transferred to the numbered Petri dishes
steps.searchable_summary: Transfer sieved material to Petri dishes

steps.description: which were then placed in an oven and kept there for a minimum of 8h at a temperature of 70-80 °C.
steps.searchable_summary: Dry sediment material in an oven

steps.description: While adding carbon tetrachloride, in the volume of three times more than the dried sediment volume
steps.searchable_summary: Add carbon tetrachloride to dried sediment

steps.description: we stirred the mixture vigorously with a glass rod
steps.searchable_summary: Stir sediment-CCl4 mixture

steps.description: After pouring the liquid on the paper filters
steps.searchable_summary: Pour liquid from mixture onto paper filters

steps.description: and drying them under standard conditions
steps.searchable_summary: Dry paper filters with separated organisms
outputs: Dried paper filters with separated organisms

steps.description: we separated the organisms
steps.searchable_summary: Separate organisms from dried filters
inputs: Dried paper filters with separated organisms

steps.description: and counted them.
steps.searchable_summary: Count separated organisms

steps.description: Meiofauna were separated and counted using stereomicroscope
steps.searchable_summary: Separate and count meiofauna using a stereomicroscope

steps.description: and referring to the published identification keys (Loeblich and Teppan, 1988)
steps.searchable_summary: Identify meiofauna using identification keys

methods.description: This method describes the statistical procedures used to analyze the collected environmental and biological data, including correlation analysis, normality testing, non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kruskall-Wallis H, Spearman Rank Correlation), ANOVA, and diversity index calculation (Shannon index).

steps.description: correlations were performed using the average data calculated for each site
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate average data for each site

steps.description: Using the Statistical Package for Social Sciences SPSS v20, (IBM Corp., Armonk. NY), correlations were performed... to determine the relationships between target communities in the sediments with different tested parameters.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform correlation analysis between community data and environmental parameters using SPSS

steps.description: The normal distribution of the data were tested using Kolmogorov-Smirnov test.
steps.searchable_summary: Test data normality using Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
outputs: Normality test results (p-values from Kolmogorov-Smirnov test)

steps.description: The non-parametric Spearman Rank Correlation Coefficient (r s ) was used due to the data being not normally distributed... Spearman rank correlation coefficient analysis was applied to test the significant relationships between heavy metal concentration and morphological variables (e.g. weight and total body length)
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Spearman Rank Correlation for non-normally distributed data (heavy metals vs morphology)
inputs: Non-normally distributed data (heavy metal concentrations, morphological variables)

steps.description: In case of non-normal data distribution, we used Mann-Whitney U... tests to investigate intra...stations measured factors like heavy metal concentration and total organic matter.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Mann-Whitney U test for non-normal intra-station factor comparisons
inputs: Non-normally distributed intra-station data (e.g., heavy metal concentration, total organic matter)
outputs: Mann-Whitney U test results (U statistic, p-value)

steps.description: In case of non-normal data distribution, we used... Kruskall-Wallis H tests to investigate... interstations measured factors like heavy metal concentration and total organic matter.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Kruskall-Wallis H test for non-normal inter-station factor comparisons
inputs: Non-normally distributed inter-station data (e.g., heavy metal concentration, total organic matter)
outputs: Kruskall-Wallis H test results (H statistic, p-value)

steps.description: One-way ANOVA was used to compare the significant difference of the measured environmental factors among the stations.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform One-way ANOVA to compare environmental factors among stations

steps.description: The association of the Foraminifera diversity and density in different samples were investigated using Pearson correlation test.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Pearson correlation test for Foraminifera diversity and density association

steps.description: Shanon index of diversity was used to determine Foraminifera and Ostracods diversity in different sampling stations (Magurran, 1988).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate Shannon diversity index for Foraminifera and Ostracods
outputs: Shannon diversity index values per station
